nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the terms "pudendal neuralgia" and clarifies it is "nerve pain" affecting the pudendal nerve distribution (perineum/pelvic floor), often worse with sitting.

Elicits key history features supporting pudendal neuralgia: perineal/pelvic pain, sitting intolerance, sensory symptoms (burning/tingling), triggers (cycling/prolonged sitting), and functional impact (sleep, sexual function, bowel/bladder symptoms).

Screens for red flags requiring urgent evaluation/referral: fever, rapidly progressive neurologic deficits, saddle anesthesia with acute urinary retention/incontinence, severe unremitting night pain, unexplained weight loss, cancer history, recent major trauma, or signs of infection.

Performs or verbalizes an appropriate focused exam plan (pelvic/perineal neuro exam, assessment of pelvic floor tenderness/trigger points, back/hip exam) and acknowledges limitations if exam cannot be performed.
Provides a treatment explanation that explicitly includes "gabapentin" and "pelvic floor PT" as components of multimodal management.

Completes required safety checks before prescribing: verifies medication allergies (including prior reaction details), checks contraindications/cautions (e.g., renal impairment requiring dose adjustment, pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, severe sedation/fall risk), and checks for clinically significant drug interactions (especially other CNS depressants).

Counsels on gabapentin use and safety: start-low/go-slow titration, common adverse effects (drowsiness, dizziness, edema), avoidance of alcohol/other sedatives when possible, driving/operating machinery caution, and not stopping abruptly without guidance.

Offers nonpharmacologic and behavioral strategies: sitting modifications (cushion/avoid prolonged sitting), activity modification (avoid cycling if triggering), bowel regimen if constipation contributes, and stress/sleep strategies.
Sets expectations: symptom improvement may take days to weeks; treatment often requires iterative adjustments; emphasizes follow-up and reassessment of pain and function.
Uses patient-centered communication: validates pain experience, invites questions, and confirms understanding using teach-back or equivalent.
